topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the faded beige of the campus bulletin board, felt like a physical blow. I traced the outline of the closing date with a fingertip, the cheap paper crinkling under the pressure. My chest felt tight, a cold, heavy knot tightening with every line of text detailing the liquidation of resources, the shuttering of lecture halls. I walked back to my dorm room, the usual hum of student life replaced by an echoing silence that seemed to amplify the finality of the news. I sat on the edge of my bed, staring at the peeling paint on the wall, a strange, hollow ache settling deep in my stomach. I picked up an old, dog-eared textbook, the weight of it grounding me momentarily, but the knowledge that this chapter—this entire structure of my young adulthood—was being ripped out from under me left me feeling unsteady, like a ship adrift without a compass.

[story 2]

"So, it's really happening?" I managed, the words coming out thin and reedy, barely audible above the distant sound of campus maintenance crews. Professor Albright, usually so booming with enthusiasm, looked down at his notes, his face etched with a weariness that mirrored my own. I watched him pack his desk, his movements slow, deliberate, as if afraid to disturb the fragile atmosphere of the room. A sudden wave of profound sadness washed over me, making my throat feel thick. It wasn't just the loss of classes; it was the loss of the shared history, the late-night study sessions fueled by bad coffee, the feeling of belonging woven into the very brick and mortar of this place. I didn't move to help pack anything; instead, I just stood there, absorbing the quiet despair radiating from everyone around me, my shoulders slumping slightly as if the weight of the impending emptiness was too much to bear alone.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the financial projections, trying to find a loophole, a reason to argue, but the numbers were concrete, unyielding. Each figure represented a piece of my future dissolving into dust. My hands began to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. The air in the administrative office was stale, smelling faintly of old paper and disuse. When the dean finally spoke, his voice was flat, devoid of the usual institutional warmth. I felt a strange, almost paralyzing sense of helplessness. It wasn't anger that rose, just a deep, pervasive sorrow—the recognition that a significant part of my trajectory, the easy path I’d envisioned, was simply being erased by bureaucratic decree. I leaned back in my chair, closing my eyes, letting the sheer magnitude of the shutdown wash over me, leaving me feeling utterly drained and profoundly small.
